Granite Ridge Resources’ Chief Financial Officer, Ronald Kyle Kettler, has signaled confidence in the company by purchasing 6,000 shares of its stock in a recent transaction. The buy, valued at $30,480, highlights a notable insider investment from a key executive overseeing the firm’s financial strategy.

Recent Updates on GRNT stock

Granite Ridge Resources shares reacted to Q1 results that showed strong production and cash flow but a headline net loss driven mainly by non‑cash hedge mark‑to‑market losses and an impairment, plus revenue slightly below consensus. Analysts adjusted price targets chiefly because weaker Waha gas prices, higher Permian operating costs, and softer realized oil and gas prices compressed near‑term margins, even as the company maintained peer‑leading profitability, low leverage, disciplined 2026 growth guidance, and ongoing dividends and acquisitions that support a longer‑term free‑cash‑flow transition.

Overall score reflects weakened recent financial results (revenue decline and a swing to losses) offset by conservative leverage and strong operating cash flow. Technical indicators are broadly neutral with slight softness. Valuation is supported by a high dividend yield, but the negative P/E underscores current earnings weakness. Earnings-call guidance is moderately positive due to reduced capital intensity and a clear plan to reach sustainable free cash flow in 2027, tempered by commodity-price and cost headwinds.

data.title: Chagee Holdings Signals Turnaround in Earnings Call
data.description:
Chagee Holdings Limited Unsponsored ADR ((CHA)) has held its Q1 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Memorial Day Sale – Claim 70% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock trusted, data-backed investing tools with TipRanks Premium, from analyst ratings and forecasts to breaking news and portfolio analysis.
  • Discover high-conviction stock picks and new investing opportunities with the TipRanks Smart Investor Newsletter

Chagee Holdings’ latest earnings call struck a cautiously upbeat tone, with management highlighting a sharp sequential recovery in revenue, GMV and margins after a weak prior quarter. Executives portrayed the business as regaining operational momentum, driven by product innovation and overseas growth, while acknowledging that year-on-year same-store softness and margin compression remain headwinds.

Revenue and GMV Rebound Signals Operational Momentum

Chagee reported Q1 revenue of RMB 3,546 million, up 4.5% year-over-year and a robust 19.2% quarter-over-quarter, suggesting a solid demand recovery. Total GMV reached RMB 7,917.8 million, rising 8.1% sequentially, which the company framed as evidence that recent marketing and product initiatives are gaining traction across its network.

Margins Swing Back to Profit as Efficiency Improves

The quarter marked a clear profitability turnaround, with GAAP net income of RMB 447.7 million and non-GAAP net income of RMB 506.7 million after an operating loss in the prior quarter. Operating income climbed to RMB 547.2 million, translating to a 15.4% margin, while non-GAAP operating margin reached 17.1%, and non-GAAP net margin improved sharply to 14.3% from 3.4% in Q4.

Overseas Expansion Drives Rapid International GMV Growth

International operations remained a bright spot, with overseas GMV at RMB 426.4 million, surging 139% year-over-year and 14.6% quarter-over-quarter. The overseas teahouse footprint expanded to 374 locations, with strong presences in Malaysia, Singapore, Indonesia, Thailand and an emerging beachhead in the U.S., underscoring Chagee’s ambition to build a global tea brand.

Network Scale and Membership Flywheel Strengthen

The global teahouse network grew to 7,531 locations, up 12.7% year-over-year, including 7,157 in Greater China and 374 overseas, highlighting continued store expansion. Registered members reached 248 million with nearly 50 million active, up 11% quarter-over-quarter, and a steady 42.3% repurchase rate, with customers making two or more purchases driving over 76% of orders.

Cost Discipline Supports Operational Efficiency Gains

Management pointed to tighter expense control, as the non-GAAP G&A ratio fell 8.1 percentage points sequentially to 11.6% and sales and marketing expenses dropped 3.6 points to 8.6% of revenue. Other operating costs decreased 7.8% to RMB 159 million, supporting the margin rebound and suggesting early benefits from the company’s efficiency push.

Product Innovation and Marketing Spark Demand

Chagee launched 12 new products in the quarter, with its Da Hong Pao line and Caramel Pour Latte delivering above-average GMV, cup contribution and repeat purchases while attracting new customers. Campaigns such as a morning buy-one-get-one offer doubled the morning card share, and localized drinks like Caramel Oolong and Hojicha Genmai posted strong early sales, notably about 440 daily cups per store in Singapore in week one.

Company-Owned Teahouses Power Revenue Mix Shift

Revenue from company-owned teahouses jumped to RMB 802.1 million, up 230.4% year-over-year, reflecting aggressive expansion and store conversions to the self-operated model. By quarter end, Chagee operated 790 company-owned locations, a strategy management argues will deepen control over customer experience and capture more unit-level economics despite higher operating costs.

Buyback Plan Underscores Confidence and Balance Sheet Strength

The board approved a share repurchase authorization of up to USD 150 million in ADS over 12 months, signaling confidence in long-term value and providing a potential cushion for the stock. The company closed the quarter with RMB 7,146.3 million in cash, cash equivalents, restricted cash and time deposits, giving it ample firepower for both capital returns and expansion.

Same-Store GMV Still Under Pressure Year-over-Year

Despite the sequential turnaround, overall same-store GMV remained down roughly 16% year-over-year, underscoring ongoing demand normalization challenges. Management emphasized that trends are improving, with same-store GMV up about 9.5 percentage points versus the previous quarter and Greater China same-store performance improving by 9.4 percentage points.

Margins Improve Sequentially but Lag Last Year

Non-GAAP net margin reached 14.3% in Q1, a sharp rebound from the prior quarter but notably below the 20.0% posted a year earlier, a 5.7-point decline. Executives linked the year-on-year compression to a different revenue mix, heavier investment in company-owned stores and ongoing promotional and innovation spending to support growth.

Higher G&A and Company-Owned Costs Reflect Scaling Pain

G&A expenses rose to RMB 462.0 million, up 30.9% year-over-year, pushing non-GAAP G&A to 11.6% of revenue versus 10.4% a year ago as the platform scales. Operating costs for company-owned teahouses surged 216.6% to RMB 497.2 million, reflecting the larger self-operated base and putting more pressure on the company to drive higher throughput and efficiency at these locations.

Franchisee Revenue Declines as Model Shifts

Net revenues from franchisee teahouses fell to RMB 2,743.9 million from RMB 3,149.9 million a year ago, a decline of about 12.9% as Chagee transitioned to a GMV-based revenue-sharing model. Management framed this as a structural shift in how value is shared with partners rather than a sign of franchise system weakness, but it does weigh on reported top line growth.

Cash Balance Erodes and Non-Cash Charges Distort GAAP

Cash and equivalents, including restricted cash and time deposits, slipped to RMB 7,146.3 million from RMB 7,892.4 million at year-end, a roughly 9.4% decline that narrows short-term liquidity headroom. Share-based compensation of RMB 59 million also widened the gap between GAAP and non-GAAP results and contributed to a higher effective tax rate of 21.2%, up from 19% a year earlier.

Guidance Highlights Steady Execution and Disciplined Growth

Looking ahead, management guided to an operations-first strategy focused on strengthening products, service, store environment, customer experience and value, with a steady pace of new launches including tea lattes and special deals. The company plans continued membership upgrades, quality-focused expansion in Greater China, steady international growth and disciplined capital deployment, underpinned by its current margins, cash position and the newly approved buyback program.

Chagee’s earnings call painted a picture of a company regaining its footing, with stronger sequential growth, improved profitability and rapid overseas traction offsetting lingering same-store and margin pressures versus last year. For investors, the key narrative is a transition toward a more company-owned, innovation-led model, backed by a sizable cash buffer and buyback support, but still navigating near-term volatility in core-store performance.

data.title: Argosy Property Earnings Call: Stability Amid Headwinds
data.description:
Argosy Property ((NZ:ARG)) has held its Q4 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Memorial Day Sale – Claim 70% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock trusted, data-backed investing tools with TipRanks Premium, from analyst ratings and forecasts to breaking news and portfolio analysis.
  • Discover high-conviction stock picks and new investing opportunities with the TipRanks Smart Investor Newsletter

Argosy Property’s latest earnings call painted a cautiously balanced picture for investors. Management highlighted steady growth in income, stronger interest cover, and a healthier balance sheet, yet repeatedly pointed to macro uncertainty, rising costs, and stubborn vacancies that will weigh on near‑term momentum. The overall tone was pragmatic rather than upbeat, with risk management clearly taking priority over aggressive growth.

Revenue and Net Property Income Growth

Argosy reported gross property income of $137.5 million, up 3.6% year on year, with net property income rising 3.3% to $120.8 million. This modest but consistent top‑line growth suggests the core portfolio is still performing despite softer leasing markets and higher vacancy in some assets.

Distributable Income and Per‑Share Metrics

Gross distributable income climbed 9.8% to $70.4 million, while net distributable income per share rose 7.1% to $0.0705. The per‑share uplift indicates that earnings growth is feeding through to shareholders, even as the dividend remains flat, providing some buffer against inflation eroding real returns.

Funds From Operations Improvement

Funds from operations increased 8.3% to $59.1 million, with FFO per share improving from $0.0643 to $0.0685. This points to healthier recurring cash earnings from the underlying portfolio, strengthening Argosy’s ability to sustain payouts and absorb shocks in a more volatile operating environment.

Portfolio Valuation and Revaluation Gains

The investment property portfolio grew by $94 million to $2.2 billion, including a $4.4 million gain on two held‑for‑sale assets. These valuation gains reflect both asset quality and market resilience, giving Argosy more balance sheet flexibility at a time when capital recycling is central to its strategy.

Balance Sheet Strength and Interest Rate Management

Debt to total assets stood at 37.2% at year‑end and has eased to just over 36% post‑settlements, while the weighted average cost of debt fell to 4.6% from 5.1%. Interest cover improved to 2.7 times and fixed‑rate cover rose to 74%, underscoring a deliberate move to de‑risk funding costs and protect cash flow.

Acquisitions and Development Completions

The company completed the acquisition of 291 East Tamaki Road and finished key capital works at 224 Neilson Street (Warehouse B) and the Mt Richmond development. Capital expenditure was tightly focused on these projects, indicating a shift from expansion toward optimizing and finishing existing commitments.

Sustainability Recognition and Green Demand

Argosy’s sustainability push is gaining traction, with 224 Neilson Street winning industrial gold and overall sustainability awards and Mt Richmond achieving a 6 Green Star rating. Management noted strong enquiry for 5–6 star green industrial buildings, suggesting that environmental performance is becoming a genuine leasing advantage.

Dividend Maintained and Policy Reset

The full‑year dividend was held at $0.0665 per share, with the fourth‑quarter distribution at $0.016625. The board shifted policy to target 80%–95% of FFO and suspended the dividend reinvestment plan, signalling a more conservative, cash‑focused stance aimed at making dividends sustainable rather than simply maximizing payout.

Geopolitical Uncertainty and Imported Inflation

Management highlighted geopolitical risks, including conflict‑linked pressures that reintroduce nonproductive imported inflation into the local economy. They warned of possible market disruption and a prolonged period of uncertainty that could extend beyond a year, shaping a more defensive outlook for capital deployment.

Higher Vacancy and Lease Conversion Delays

Vacancy levels are higher than Argosy would like and leasing conversion times have lengthened by more than a month. A critical lease at 224 Neilson Street remains conditional and, if secured, would now commence on 1 March next year rather than July, leaving about one and a half years of vacancy exposure on a significant asset.

Development Activity to Be Reined In

The company expects minimal development activity in coming periods due to anticipated 10%–15% construction cost inflation driven by oil, transport, and materials. With discretionary spending constrained, Argosy is prioritizing risk control and returns on existing assets over speculative new projects.

Rising Operating Costs and Expense Pressures

Net property expenses increased by $0.9 million as nonrecoverable rates in Wellington and vacancy‑related operating costs outweighed insurance savings. Maintenance costs rose by $1.4 million, reflecting higher spending on tenant fit‑outs and HVAC replacements, which will need to be offset by rent growth and tighter cost discipline.

Flat Dividend Track Record and Dilution Concerns

The dividend has been stuck at $0.0665 per share for five years, prompting shareholder questions about real income erosion against consumer price inflation. The suspension of the DRP removes a low‑friction reinvestment option, sharpening investor focus on whether earnings growth can eventually translate into higher cash returns.

Leasing Market Pressures in Industrial and Retail

Industrial leasing remains sluggish, with oversupply in some pockets and incentives drifting toward roughly 12% to secure deals. Large‑format retail is expected to struggle outside prime locations such as Albany, making asset selection and tenant quality more critical for maintaining occupancy and rental resilience.

Tenant and Asset‑Specific Vacancy Challenges

Vacancy at 101 Carlton Gore Road nearly doubled following a lease roll‑out, highlighting sensitivity in office‑style assets. Upcoming expiries, including the warehouse at 17 Mayo Road and other leases, will require active management to limit revenue leakage and avoid a further drag on occupancy metrics.

FFO Volatility and Dividend Basis Change

Argosy shifted its dividend reference from FO to FFO, arguing that FO is distorted by volatile adjustments such as amortization, leasing incentives, and below‑the‑line items. Management also cited one‑off tax and deduction timing issues, positioning FFO as a more stable measure for setting predictable distributions.

Forward‑Looking Guidance and Outlook

Guidance for FY27 keeps the dividend flat at $0.0665 per share under the new 80%–95% FFO payout policy, with the DRP still suspended. Argosy expects minimal development, rising construction costs of 10%–15%, and, if the Neilson Street lease completes, occupancy improving to about 97.2% with a WALE near 5.3 years, underpinned by a stronger, more hedged balance sheet.

Argosy’s earnings call underscored a mix of solid operating gains and genuine headwinds, leaving the near‑term story one of disciplined defense rather than rapid growth. Income and valuations are moving in the right direction and the balance sheet is better protected, but higher vacancies, cost pressures, and a flat dividend mean investors will need patience as management prioritizes stability over expansion.

data.title: Chevron Shareholders Back Board, Pay Plans at Meeting
data.description:
Memorial Day Sale – Claim 70% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock trusted, data-backed investing tools with TipRanks Premium, from analyst ratings and forecasts to breaking news and portfolio analysis.
  • Discover high-conviction stock picks and new investing opportunities with the TipRanks Smart Investor Newsletter

An update from Chevron ( (CVX) ) is now available.

At Chevron’s 2026 Annual Meeting of Stockholders held on May 27, 2026, all nominated directors were elected to one-year terms, with each receiving strong majority support from voting shareholders. Stockholders also approved the ratification of PricewaterhouseCoopers LLP as the independent auditor for 2026 and endorsed, on an advisory basis, the company’s executive compensation program.

Shareholders rejected three prominent governance and human-rights-related stockholder proposals, including a call for an independent board chair, a request for a report on indigenous peoples’ rights, and a proposal for a third-party review of Chevron’s human rights processes. The voting outcomes underscore broad investor backing for existing board leadership and pay structures, while signaling limited shareholder momentum for the proposed changes in governance and rights-related oversight at this time.

The most recent analyst rating on (CVX) stock is a Buy with a $230.00 price target. To see the full list of analyst forecasts on Chevron stock, see the CVX Stock Forecast page.

Spark’s Take on CVX Stock

According to Spark, TipRanks’ AI Analyst, CVX is a Neutral.

CVX scores as moderately attractive: the biggest support is solid underlying financial quality (especially balance-sheet strength) and a constructive earnings-call setup with reiterated guidance, production growth, and disciplined capital returns. The main offsets are weak near-term technical momentum and a demanding P/E multiple for a cyclical business, even with a strong dividend yield.

To see Spark’s full report on CVX stock, click here.

More about Chevron

Chevron is a global integrated energy company engaged primarily in the exploration, production, refining, and marketing of oil and natural gas, as well as the development of lower-carbon energy solutions. The company operates across the upstream and downstream value chain and holds a significant position among the world’s largest publicly traded energy producers and suppliers.

Average Trading Volume: 12,294,637

Technical Sentiment Signal: Buy

Current Market Cap: $363.3B

data.title: Replimune to Resubmit Priority FDA Filing for RP1
data.description:
Memorial Day Sale – Claim 70% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock trusted, data-backed investing tools with TipRanks Premium, from analyst ratings and forecasts to breaking news and portfolio analysis.
  • Discover high-conviction stock picks and new investing opportunities with the TipRanks Smart Investor Newsletter

An announcement from Replimune Group ( (REPL) ) is now available.

On May 29, 2026, Replimune announced it will shortly resubmit its Biologics License Application to the U.S. Food and Drug Administration for RP1, used in combination with nivolumab, for patients with advanced melanoma. Following what the company described as productive, collaborative discussions, Replimune and the FDA have agreed on a path for reconsideration of the filing after earlier setbacks.

The FDA has signaled it will prioritize review of the resubmission as an urgent matter, reflecting the significant unmet need among patients whose disease has progressed on prior anti-PD-1-based therapy. The application is backed by data from the IGNYTE trial and, if ultimately successful, could strengthen Replimune’s position in oncology by introducing a new option in a setting where standard checkpoint inhibitor regimens fail roughly half of patients.

The most recent analyst rating on (REPL) stock is a Sell with a $1.00 price target. To see the full list of analyst forecasts on Replimune Group stock, see the REPL Stock Forecast page.

Spark’s Take on REPL Stock

According to Spark, TipRanks’ AI Analyst, REPL is a Neutral.

The score is held down primarily by weak financial performance (no revenue, widening losses, and substantial cash burn) and bearish technicals (price well below key moving averages with negative MACD). Valuation provides limited support due to a negative P/E and no dividend. A recent debt facility amendment modestly offsets risk by extending runway and improving financing flexibility.

To see Spark’s full report on REPL stock, click here.

More about Replimune Group

Replimune Group, Inc. is a clinical-stage biotechnology company headquartered in Woburn, Mass., focused on developing novel oncolytic immunotherapies based on a proprietary HSV-1 platform. Its lead product candidate, RP1 (vusolimogene oderparepvec), is designed to enhance tumor cell killing and trigger systemic anti-tumor immune responses, with potential synergy alongside established and experimental cancer treatments.

The company’s RPx platform aims to induce immunogenic cell death and reshape the tumor microenvironment by combining direct virus-mediated tumor destruction with immune activation. Replimune is targeting a range of cancers where standard therapies, including immune checkpoint inhibitors, leave substantial unmet medical need, positioning its candidates for use both as monotherapies and in combination regimens.

Average Trading Volume: 6,183,357

Technical Sentiment Signal: Sell

Current Market Cap: $388.1M
